locked_facts: Electric field inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is zero
All excess charge resides on the outer surface of a conductor
Electric field at the surface is perpendicular to the surface and equals σ/ε₀
Surface charge density is highest at points of greatest curvature (sharp tips)
The entire conductor is an equipotential in electrostatic equilibrium
A cavity inside a conductor is shielded from external electric fields (Faraday cage)
For a spherical conductor, σ is uniform; for irregular shapes, σ is highest at sharp points
truth_statements: In the static situation the electric field is zero everywhere inside the material of the conductor
The charge on a conductor resides entirely on its outer surface
At the surface of a charged conductor the electric field is normal to the surface with magnitude σ/ε₀
Charge density is highest at the sharpest point of an irregularly shaped conductor
The interior of a conductor is shielded from external electrostatic fields — this is the basis of the Faraday cage

aha_moment.what_to_show: An irregularly shaped conductor with charge density visualised as colour intensity — the sharp tip glows brightest (highest σ) while flat regions are dimmer (lower σ). E field arrows at the tip are much longer than at flat regions.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Colour-coded charge density on the surface with the sharp tip visibly brighter and stronger E arrows there
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students assume charge distributes uniformly — seeing the dramatic concentration at the tip makes non-uniformity obvious

simulation_states.label: Neutral conductor
simulation_states.description: An irregularly shaped conductor with no charge. Field probes inside show E = 0. The conductor is a single equipotential.
simulation_states.emphasis: Inside any conductor, E = 0 in electrostatic equilibrium

simulation_states.label: Charge added
simulation_states.description: Charge is placed on the conductor. It immediately moves to the outer surface. Interior field probes still read zero. Surface charge density shown as colour map.
simulation_states.emphasis: All charge migrates to the outer surface — none stays inside

simulation_states.label: Charge concentration at tip
simulation_states.description: Colour intensity map shows σ is highest at the sharp tip. E field arrows at the tip are 3-4× longer than at flat regions. Lightning rod effect explained.
simulation_states.emphasis: Sharp tip = high σ = strong E — this is why lightning rods work

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts conductor shape (more/less pointed). A Faraday cage demo shows external field cannot penetrate. Field and charge distribution update in real time.
simulation_states.emphasis: Make the tip sharper — watch σ increase. Place inside a Faraday cage — external E vanishes.

common_misconceptions.belief: Charge distributes uniformly on any shaped conductor
common_misconceptions.correction: Charge density σ is highest at points of smallest radius of curvature (sharp tips) and lowest at flat regions
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Colour-coded σ map showing bright tip and dim flat region

common_misconceptions.belief: Electric field exists inside a charged conductor
common_misconceptions.correction: In electrostatic equilibrium, the field inside the material of a conductor is exactly zero — free electrons redistribute to cancel any internal field
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Place field probes inside — all read zero regardless of external charges
